explanation:
The input natural language states that any power failure should leave the system in a state that allows the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded. The available atomic propositions do not directly specify detailed valve states for inspiration/expiration, but do include failSafeMode, which is described as: 'the controller detects a severe condition that required an alarm to be raised and to bring the machine to a fail-safe mode (in valve closed and out valve open).' Given that in fail-safe mode, the inspiratory valve is closed and the expiratory valve is open, this matches a common medical device safety approach: if power is lost, the patient is allowed to exhale freely (minimizing the risk of barotrauma or rebreathing). Since there is an atomic proposition outValveOpen ('indicates out valve is open'), we can use this to directly represent the system being left in a state that allows unimpeded exhalation. In the absence of further atomic propositions about specific inspiration/expiration mechanisms, ensuring the expiratory valve is open suffices for 'unimpeded' exhalation (and thus overall breathing) upon power failure.

We therefore formalize: whenever there is a power failure (powerFailure), globally it follows that outValveOpen holds (the system ensures the expiratory valve remains open). This is captured with the LTL formula: G(powerFailure -> outValveOpen)

This property is the best representation with the available atomic propositions; any attempt to specify both inspiration and expiration unimpeded would require atomic propositions that specifically denote an open inspiratory valve as well, which aren't present.

The requirement is that any power failure must leave the system in a state where the patient is able to inhale and exhale without impedance. Among the provided atomic propositions, only the occurrence of 'powerFailure' is directly related to the antecedent (condition) of the natural language. For the consequent, there is no atomic proposition that explicitly or semantically encodes 'patient can inhale and exhale unimpeded'. However, the closest is the absence of the failure-safety state (failSafeMode), the patient's safety ('patientSafe'), or the absence of abnormal conditions such as 'P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp' (overpressure impeding inhalation), or the presence of 'outValveOpen' (which would allow exhalation). Since 'patientSafe' most closely maps to the general safety and unimpaired state, the best LTL approximation is that whenever a power failure occurs, it should always be the case that the patient is safe (patientSafe). This can be expressed as a response property: 'It is always the case that if a power failure happens, then globally afterwards the patient is safe'. This is captured as: G(powerFailure -> G(patientSafe)).

The requirement is: Any power failure shall leave the system in a state that allows the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded. Since we want to express in LTL that whenever a power failure occurs, the valves are arranged so that the patient can breathe (i.e., inspiratory valve open and expiratory valve open, or for this domain, more precisely, inspiratory valve is closed and expiratory valve is open—which matches the standard fail-safe position in ventilators to promote passive breathing). Reviewing the atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' is defined as 'in valve closed and out valve open', which means the arrangement that allows the patient to breathe unimpeded during failures. Thus, the property captures: Whenever a power failure occurs, the system enters and remains in the fail-safe mode, globally. So, globally, whenever powerFailure is true, failSafeMode must also be true at that point (or immediately after). This is captured by LTL formula: G(powerFailure -> failSafeMode). This translation best matches the natural language: any power failure shall leave (i.e., cause, guarantee) the system in (at least) the failSafeMode. Other properties might try to specify details about valves directly, but this atomic proposition already precisely captures the required ventilator hardware state in medical standards, so this is the most direct and correct model for the requirement.

The requirement states that if a power failure occurs, the system must remain in a state that enables unimpeded inhalation and exhalation for the patient. Mapping this to atomic propositions, we have 'powerFailure' to indicate power failure. Among atomic propositions, 'outValveOpen' means the exhalation valve is open (enabling exhalation), and '!inValveClose', i.e., inspiratory valve is not closed, aligns with inspiration being unimpeded (or more precisely, 'inValveOpen' means inspiration is allowed). To capture this in LTL, we express that globally, whenever a power failure happens, it is always the case that the out valve stays open and the in valve is not closed (i.e., open or at least not closed): G(powerFailure -> G(outValveOpen & !inValveClose)). This LTL ensures that once the power failure happens, it will remain true forever after (globally, after the failure), the system permits breathing, thus matching the “shall leave the system in a state...” clause. Prior LTL (if any) may not have enforced this global/forever-after condition or might have omitted the 'during power failure' context. This version tightly links the system state following any power failure to continual allowance of unimpeded breathing.

The natural language requirement is that 'any power failure shall leave the system in a state that allows the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded.' The closest appropriate propositions to represent 'ability to inhale and exhale unimpeded' are that 'outValveOpen' is true and 'inValveOpen' is true. From the domain, it is plausible that the fail-safe mode (where inValveClose & outValveOpen are true) allows exhalation and prevents inhalation, but only 'outValveOpen' is available as an explicit atomic proposition. Therefore, the LTL property must specify that whenever a power failure occurs, eventually the out valve opens (permitting exhalation) and the inspiratory valve does not remain closed (to avoid blocked inhalation).

Thus, the property can be formalized as: whenever 'powerFailure' occurs, eventually both 'inValveOpen' and 'outValveOpen' become true (the system allows for both inspiration and expiration pathways to be open, ensuring unimpeded breathing). In LTL: G(powerFailure -> F(inValveOpen & outValveOpen))

Alternative properties (such as only requiring outValveOpen or reliance on failSafeMode) risk not guaranteeing both inhale and exhale; only monitoring alarms or safe indicators would not precisely enforce the unimpeded breathing pathway requirement. Therefore, the present LTL best and most directly captures the specified safety requirement.

The requirement states that after any power failure, the system must be left in a state that allows the patient to inhale and exhale without restriction. Given the provided atomic propositions, there is no direct representation of 'unimpeded inhalation and exhalation'; however, the absence of 'inValveClose' (inspiratory valve closed) and 'outValveOpen' (expiratory valve open) together is a commonly used safe state in ventilator logic (see definition of 'failSafeMode'), used to allow spontaneous patient breathing. Thus, after a power failure, the system should ensure that 'failSafeMode' holds (i.e., the system is in fail-safe mode: in valve closed, out valve open). We use the 'powerFailure' atomic proposition to capture the triggering event. The LTL translation says: whenever a power failure happens, it is always (globally) followed that the system is in 'failSafeMode' (i.e., G(powerFailure -> G(failSafeMode))). This captures the idea that any power failure must leave the system in this state thereafter. Previous LTL properties could have attempted to use other atomic propositions, but based on the available atoms, 'failSafeMode' most closely matches the required safety stance described in the natural language requirement.

The requirement states that, following any power failure, the system shall allow the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded. Given the available atomic propositions, the most relevant one to patient airway access in a generic sense is 'outValveOpen', which indicates that the expiratory (exhalation) valve is open, and '!inValveClose' (i.e., the inspiratory valve is not closed) to indicate that the inspiratory path is available for inhalation. However, to capture the necessary LTL property more conservatively and simply with available indicators, we use outValveOpen as a proxy for unimpeded exhalation, and !inValveClose as a proxy for inhalation. The property should hold that, globally, whenever a power failure occurs, it is always the case (globally) afterwards that the patient continues to be able to inhale and exhale unimpeded. We don't have a direct indicator for both at once, but combining ensures that as long as at least those two are true, the path is unimpeded. Thus, whenever a power failure occurs, it is always the case that the outValve is open and the inValve is not closed. The LTL property expresses this via: G(powerFailure -> G(outValveOpen & !inValveClose)). This says: whenever power failure happens, it is globally (forever after) required that outValve is open and inspiratory valve is not closed (i.e., always both inhalation and exhalation possible).

The natural language specifies that any power failure shall leave the system in a state that allows the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded. This requires that whenever a power failure occurs (powerFailure), the critical safety condition related to the patient's unimpeded ability to breathe must be ensured after, or at least not violated during, the failure. Since there is no explicit atomic proposition for 'inhaling and exhaling unimpeded,' but based on the atomic propositions present, the closest indicator of patient safety is 'patientSafe.' Therefore, the safest and most accurate property expressible is: globally, whenever powerFailure occurs, it is always the case (globally henceforth) that patientSafe is true. This is captured in LTL as G(powerFailure -> G(patientSafe)). This property ensures that after any power failure, the system must maintain a state where the patient is safe (i.e., not impeded in breathing), according to the available atomic propositions.

The input natural language specifies that after any power failure, the system must end up in a condition where the patient is able to inhale and exhale without any obstruction. Given the atomic propositions, a reasonable encoding of this is to require that whenever a power failure occurs, it is always the case from then on that the 'outValveOpen' is true (to allow exhalation) and the 'inValveClose' is true (to block inhalation), which is indicated in the fail-safe mode per the atomic proposition descriptions. Alternatively, if 'failSafeMode' means both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' together, we use that atomic proposition directly. However, 'unimpeded' breathing (free inhalation and exhalation) may more closely correspond to the requirement that neither 'inValveClose' nor 'outValveClose' is true, but we have no 'outValveClose' atomic proposition, only 'inValveClose'. Thus, the best approximation is to enforce 'failSafeMode' after power failure. So we write: Whenever powerFailure occurs, globally from then on failSafeMode must hold. This property ensures the state transition after powerFailure allows patient to breathe unimpeded according to the available propositions.

The requirement states that after any power failure, the system should remain in a state where the patient is able to inhale and exhale unimpeded. Given the available atomic propositions, we do not have explicit indicators for 'unimpeded inhaling and exhaling.' However, we can infer that if the expiratory valve is open (outValveOpen) and the inspiratory valve is not closed (¬inValveClose), this enables possible air flow both ways. Therefore, after any occurrence of powerFailure, it must always be the case that either the outValveOpen is true and inValveClose is false, ensuring that the patient can breathe. The property is thus encoded by: G(powerFailure -> F (outValveOpen & !inValveClose)). This says globally whenever powerFailure occurs, eventually (i.e., after or immediately after), the system ensures the patient's breath is unimpeded as inferred by those valve conditions. No previous LTL property is present to compare against; this property best matches the available atomic propositions and the intent of the natural language specification.
